title: Foundations: Posture and Clean Tone
subtitle: Set the hands before you chase speed.
goal: Build a stable sitting position, relaxed fretting hand, and a clean open-string tone with the pick.
warmup: Roll the shoulders down and let both wrists stay loose.
Play six open strings slowly and listen for equal volume.
lessonSteps: Check pick angle and keep only 2-3 mm of the tip showing.
Fret one note at the fifth fret on each string and release without squeaks.
Alternate down-up on one string at 60 BPM for one minute without tensing up.
practiceLoop: 8 bars open strings, 8 bars fretted notes, repeat three times.
Record one pass and listen for weak notes or scratchy attacks.
coachNotes: Small relaxed motions beat big dramatic ones.
If the note buzzes, move closer to the fret before pressing harder.
checkpoint: You can play all six strings evenly with no accidental muting.

title: Left-Hand Anchors
subtitle: Teach the hand where home is.
goal: Learn fingertip placement, thumb support, and simple finger independence on frets 1-4.
warmup: Tap fingers 1-2-3-4 on a table with even spacing.
Play chromatic notes on the first string from fret 1 to 4.
lessonSteps: Keep the thumb behind the neck and the palm open.
Play 1-2-3-4 on every string, then reverse 4-3-2-1.
Lift fingers only a little above the string so the hand stays calm.
practiceLoop: Run the exercise across all six strings twice at 55 BPM.
Rest, then repeat at 65 BPM with the same clean sound.
coachNotes: The hand should feel balanced, not squeezed.
Watch the knuckles: they should stay rounded instead of collapsing.
checkpoint: You can complete a full 1-2-3-4 cycle without extra finger movement.

title: Open Chords Core Set
subtitle: Build the first four chords that unlock real songs.
goal: Memorize C, G, Am, and Fmaj7 shapes and switch between them in time.
warmup: Name each chord before you fret it.
Place each shape once, then release and reset.
lessonSteps: Build the shapes from the lowest finger to the highest finger.
Strum each chord four times with slow downstrokes.
Switch C-G-Am-Fmaj7 at 50 BPM and stay ahead of the beat.
practiceLoop: Play the four-chord loop for 3 minutes without stopping.
Circle the change that feels slowest and isolate only that move for 90 seconds.
coachNotes: Do not stare at every finger for too long; train quick snapshots.
A half-clean change in time is better than a perfect change that arrives late.
checkpoint: You can keep the progression moving for 16 bars.

title: Chord Change Engine
subtitle: Turn shapes into usable timing.
goal: Improve the mechanics of switching between common open chords without freezing the strumming hand.
warmup: Air-switch between two chord shapes without touching the strings.
Mute the strings and strum steady quarter notes.
lessonSteps: Practice anchor-finger moves, especially C-Am and G-Em style motions.
Use the one-minute change test for C to G, G to D, and Am to Fmaj7.
Count aloud while changing so the rhythm never disappears.
practiceLoop: 30 seconds one chord, 30 seconds the other, 1 minute switching.
Finish with a four-chord loop at a slow song tempo.
coachNotes: The right hand is the drummer; keep it moving.
Look for fingers that can stay down or move together as a pair.
checkpoint: You can land the next chord by beat 1 more than 80% of the time.

title: Strumming Basics
subtitle: Make the right hand feel like one smooth wheel.
goal: Develop a relaxed quarter-note and eighth-note strumming motion with simple accents.
warmup: Clap quarter notes with a metronome at 72 BPM.
Strum muted strings with only downstrokes for 16 counts.
lessonSteps: Add down-up eighth notes while the wrist stays loose.
Accent beat 2 and beat 4 without changing the tempo.
Move across one chord for two bars, then a second chord for two bars.
practiceLoop: Play one minute of eighth notes, rest 20 seconds, then repeat three times.
Finish with a simple pop pattern on C and G.
coachNotes: The motion should continue even on rests; just miss the strings.
Let the forearm guide the groove and the wrist keep it soft.
checkpoint: Your eighth-note groove stays even for a full minute.

title: Arpeggio Clarity
subtitle: Separate the strings so each chord sounds expensive.
goal: Pick through open chord shapes cleanly and keep string-crossing controlled.
warmup: Play thumb-index-middle-ring on open strings slowly.
Mute all strings and practice crossing from string 6 to 1 without catching extra strings.
lessonSteps: Use Am and C shapes for simple broken-chord picking.
Keep the pick path short and close to the next string.
Repeat a four-note arpeggio until each note speaks at equal volume.
practiceLoop: Two bars Am arpeggio, two bars C arpeggio, repeat five times.
Record one pass and listen for missed strings or volume jumps.
coachNotes: Slow arpeggios reveal every tiny movement.
If one note pops out too hard, lighten only that stroke instead of changing everything.
checkpoint: You can arpeggiate two chord shapes without stray strings ringing.

title: Power Chords and Palm Muting
subtitle: Get the rock rhythm engine online.
goal: Learn movable power-chord shapes and basic palm muting for tighter riffs.
warmup: Mute the low E string near the bridge and test the tone.
Slide a fifth-shape power chord between frets 3, 5, and 7.
lessonSteps: Play root-fifth shapes on the 6th and 5th string sets.
Alternate between open chugs and full power chords.
Use short rests so the groove breathes instead of blurring together.
practiceLoop: Play 8 bars of chugs plus 8 bars of voiced chords, then repeat.
Finish with a simple two-riff mini etude at 90 BPM.
coachNotes: Palm muting is about placement, not pressing hard.
When the chord moves, the muting hand should barely change.
checkpoint: You can keep power chords tight and rhythmically clear at 90 BPM.

title: 12-Bar Blues Builder
subtitle: Connect rhythm guitar to a real form.
goal: Understand the I-IV-V structure and play a steady shuffle-friendly rhythm part in A.
warmup: Count a 12-bar cycle out loud before playing.
Strum A7, D7, and E7 once each and say their bar numbers.
lessonSteps: Play the full 12-bar form with slow quarter-note strums.
Add a swing feel by slightly delaying the offbeat.
Finish each chorus cleanly and restart without losing count.
practiceLoop: Play three full choruses back to back.
On the final chorus, accent the chord changes more clearly.
coachNotes: If you lose the form, stop and count the bars again.
The goal is not flashy playing; it is strong form awareness.
checkpoint: You can complete three clean 12-bar choruses without getting lost.

title: Major Scale Map: One Position
subtitle: Start seeing melody as a shape, not random notes.
goal: Memorize one major scale pattern and connect finger numbers to sound and direction.
warmup: Speak the scale degrees 1 through 7 before playing.
Play the shape ascending once and descending once very slowly.
lessonSteps: Use strict alternate picking through the position.
Pause on scale degrees 1, 3, and 5 so they sound like targets.
Improvise a four-note answer phrase after each ascent.
practiceLoop: Two clean reps of the scale, then one free phrase.
Repeat that cycle six times.
coachNotes: Do not race the scale. Learn the lane lines first.
Landing on chord tones makes even simple lines sound musical.
checkpoint: You can play the full shape from memory and name the root notes.

title: Minor Pentatonic Phrasing
subtitle: Make the box shape start sounding like music.
goal: Use the minor pentatonic scale for short call-and-response phrases with bends and slides.
warmup: Play the box shape once without any expressive techniques.
Sing a tiny two-note phrase before playing it.
lessonSteps: Add slides into target notes on beats 1 and 3.
Introduce one quarter-step feel bend, then one full controlled bend.
Leave space after every phrase so it sounds intentional.
practiceLoop: Play four 2-bar phrases, rest one bar, then repeat the cycle.
End by recording a 30-second mini solo.
coachNotes: Space is part of the phrase.
A controlled bend with good intonation is worth more than five fast notes.
checkpoint: Your phrases sound distinct instead of like one long scale run.

title: Barre Chord Entry
subtitle: Build strength without fighting the guitar.
goal: Learn how to set up the index finger, wrist angle, and support needed for clean major and minor barre chords.
warmup: Barre two strings first before trying all six.
Shake the hand out between attempts.
lessonSteps: Place the index slightly on its side and stack the other fingers after it.
Test only the middle four strings first, then add the outer strings.
Move between one open chord and one barre chord to keep the hand relaxed.
practiceLoop: 20 seconds holding shape, 20 seconds rest, six rounds.
Finish with a slow four-bar progression using one barre shape.
coachNotes: Barre chords come from leverage and angle, not from panic squeezing.
If the hand burns too quickly, reset your thumb and elbow position.
checkpoint: One full barre shape rings clearly at least once in three tries.

title: Triad Navigation
subtitle: Shrink big chord shapes into elegant rhythm parts.
goal: Find and use major and minor triads on the top three strings for cleaner pop and worship comping.
warmup: Play three-note shapes on strings 1-2-3 only.
Say root-third-fifth while fretting each inversion.
lessonSteps: Map one major chord across three inversions.
Move the same shape family to a new key.
Comp a simple progression using only top-string triads.
practiceLoop: Two minutes mapping inversions, two minutes using them in a progression.
End with a recorded 16-bar comping pass.
coachNotes: Small shapes sound polished because they leave space for the vocal.
Voice-leading matters more than big strums in this lesson.
checkpoint: You can comp a four-chord progression using triads only.

title: Fingerstyle Patterns
subtitle: Separate bass and melody with control.
goal: Develop a steady thumb pulse and simple fingerstyle roll patterns over open-chord shapes.
warmup: Tap the thumb pulse on beats 1 and 3 while counting.
Play bass notes only on a C chord.
lessonSteps: Add index and middle fingers to create a repeating roll.
Keep the bass notes steady even when the top notes change.
Switch between two chord shapes without losing the thumb pulse.
practiceLoop: 90 seconds one pattern, rest, then repeat on a second chord pair.
Finish with an 8-bar fingerstyle etude.
coachNotes: The thumb is your drummer here.
If the pattern collapses, simplify the top notes but keep the bass steady.
checkpoint: Your thumb pulse stays consistent while the upper notes move.

title: Rhythm Accents and Ghost Strums
subtitle: Turn a plain pattern into a groove.
goal: Use accents, ghost strums, and muted hits to create a more modern rhythm feel.
warmup: Mute the strings and count 1-and-2-and-3-and-4-and.
Accent only beat 2 and 4 for one minute.
lessonSteps: Add ghost strums on selected offbeats.
Use left-hand release to create short percussive hits.
Apply the pattern to a two-chord loop without speeding up.
practiceLoop: Play 16 bars with accents, then 16 bars with accents plus ghost strokes.
Record one take and check if the groove still feels relaxed.
coachNotes: Muted motion should feel light and bouncy, not stiff.
The groove comes from contrast between loud and soft.
checkpoint: Your strumming pattern feels dynamic instead of flat.

title: Dynamics and Tone Shaping
subtitle: Learn to sound bigger without playing more notes.
goal: Control attack, pickup position, and palm muting depth to shape tone inside one progression.
warmup: Play one chord quietly, then medium, then strong.
Move the picking hand from bridge area toward the neck and compare tone.
lessonSteps: Use the same riff with three different attack levels.
Alternate full open strums with lightly muted strums.
Decide where the chorus should feel wider and where the verse should feel tighter.
practiceLoop: Perform a 16-bar arrangement with soft verse and stronger chorus.
Repeat until the contrast is obvious on playback.
coachNotes: Dynamics are a musical choice, not an accident.
If every section is loud, nothing feels exciting.
checkpoint: The same progression now has a clear verse and chorus personality.

title: Lead Lines with Target Notes
subtitle: Aim phrases at harmony instead of wandering.
goal: Outline chord changes by landing on strong chord tones inside simple lead phrases.
warmup: Play a backing loop and speak the chord names ahead of time.
Find one safe target note for each chord.
lessonSteps: Start each phrase with a scale note and end it on a chord tone.
Use slides and bends only if the final pitch lands cleanly.
Create four phrases that each point clearly at the next chord.
practiceLoop: Loop a four-chord progression and play one idea per chord.
Record and mark which phrase sounds most connected to the harmony.
coachNotes: Good phrasing sounds like you knew where the chord was going.
Target notes make simple lines sound informed.
checkpoint: You can hear and hit one strong chord tone per chord change.

title: Double Stops and Hook Writing
subtitle: Add width to pop and indie lead parts.
goal: Use thirds, sixths, and unison-style hooks to build memorable guitar lines.
warmup: Play two-note shapes slowly and listen for tuning.
Slide into a double stop from one fret below.
lessonSteps: Build a short hook using repeated rhythm and changing intervals.
Alternate single-note pickup notes with double-stop resolutions.
Keep the phrase singable and repeatable.
practiceLoop: Write a 2-bar hook and loop it over a progression for 3 minutes.
Make one cleaner variation instead of many random edits.
coachNotes: Hooks win because they are clear, not because they are complex.
Check tuning on every double stop before chasing speed.
checkpoint: You finish with one repeatable hook that sounds song-ready.

title: Chord Melody Basics
subtitle: Let the top note carry the story.
goal: Combine simple chord voicings with a melody note on top for solo arrangement practice.
warmup: Sing the melody phrase first.
Find the melody notes on the top two strings.
lessonSteps: Harmonize the melody with the smallest chord shape possible.
Keep the melody as the highest sounding note every time.
Play slowly enough that the listener clearly hears the top voice.
practiceLoop: Arrange four bars, then repeat them until they feel settled.
Add the next four bars only after the first phrase is stable.
coachNotes: The melody is the boss.
Choose easy voicings over ambitious voicings that bury the tune.
checkpoint: The top note is always clear and the harmony stays underneath it.

title: Pocket Session Guitar
subtitle: Practice sounding reliable inside a band mix.
goal: Lock a rhythm part to the click with clean starts, clean stops, and consistent subdivision.
warmup: Clap quarter notes and eighth notes with the click.
Mute the strings and strum only the rhythm pattern.
lessonSteps: Play a two-bar rhythm figure for 90 seconds without fills.
Use rests and dead strokes exactly as written.
Restart instantly if the groove drifts.
practiceLoop: Three rounds of 90 seconds steady playing with 30 seconds rest.
Last round: record it and check whether your stops are as clean as your strums.
coachNotes: Reliable rhythm guitar sounds simple from the outside and disciplined from the inside.
The audience feels your timing before they notice your chord voicings.
checkpoint: Your rhythm part feels stable enough to sit under a singer.

title: Performance Polish Set
subtitle: Turn practice material into stage-ready playing.
goal: Combine tone, timing, transitions, and dynamics in one final performance-focused lesson.
warmup: Warm up with the one exercise that usually fixes your weak spot fastest.
Play your chosen progression once with no pressure just to settle in.
lessonSteps: Run a full arrangement with intro, verse, chorus, and ending.
Mark one weak transition and repair it in isolation.
Perform the full take again without stopping for small mistakes.
practiceLoop: Two full takes with one targeted repair between them.
Choose the better take and write one note about what still needs work.
coachNotes: A polished performance is controlled energy.
Do not break the flow for tiny slips; finish the phrase like a musician.
checkpoint: You can perform one complete piece with shape, confidence, and control.
